headline: Eight States, One River, No Agreement That Works
context: Selangor's raw water reserves dropped below 30% in February 2026 while interstate water transfer negotiations with Pahang stalled for the ninth consecutive year.

cards.big: KL has 8 million people and no guaranteed water supply.
cards.sub: The Pahang-Selangor raw water transfer: agreed in principle since 2017, unsigned.

cards.big: Water is a state matter. Demand is a federal-capital problem.
cards.sub: The National Water Services Commission has advisory power only. States can ignore it.

cards.big: Non-revenue water: 33%. One-third of treated water never reaches a tap.
cards.sub: Pipe replacement rate: 1.2% per year. Full replacement: 83 years at current pace.

cards.big: Selangor's water tariff has not changed since 2006.
cards.sub: Twenty years of subsidized rates destroyed investment incentives for infrastructure.

cards.big: The crisis is not rainfall. It is governance architecture.
cards.sub: Enough water falls. Not enough reaches people. That is a system failure.

cards.big: Interstate water requires federal authority that the constitution does not clearly provide. The structural gap is the crisis.
